![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi_webp/HCL2qGQdp8E/maxresdefault.webp)
# [A Guide to Breakbeat Structure and Balance - Deep Jungle Style](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HCL2qGQdp8E)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Signal Freud](http://www.youtube.com/@signalfreud)
**Uploaded at**: 2025-09-03T07:14:05-07:00
**Published at**: 2025-09-03T07:14:05-07:00
**Length**: 16:56
**Views**: 218
**Likes**: 18
**Category**: Music

## Description

```
Deep Jungle type stuff here. A Jungle DnB Breakbeat tutorial for the real ones.

Analog Feedback Bass Drones: https://www.patreon.com/c/signalfreud/shop

Signal Freud: taplink.cc/signalfreud

Gick n' Morden: taplink.cc/gicknmorden

Patreon: https://www.patreon.com/c/SignalFreud

Intro - 0:00
Sub Divisions and Patterns - 3:24
The Trick of The Trade - 5:49
Call and Response - 8:29
Return to Theme - 9:15
Easy Trick for Variation - 10:03
Long Transitions - 11:12
Finished Section - 15:10
How to Analyze Fast Breaks - 15:35
Outro - 16:22

#dnb #jungle #musicproduction
```

## Transcript

Hey everybody. We're going to be covering a couple different things today and it's all going to add up to maintaining balance and contributing to the dancability of your track. Overall, it's it's grooviness, right? Originally, I explained this while chopping each individual section, but I think it's going to be easier to show you guys where I copy and pasted, where I made a variation, and what's going on with the baseline without writing it in the process. Okay. Well, let's just listen to it first. I'm gonna play the first eight bars and then I'm gonna play it again with the baseline all the way through. [Music] Right. Simple enough with the baseline. [Music] I'm a [Music] [Music] Hey. [Music] Hey. Hey. [Music] [Music] And then here is the unfinished part. Why is it unfinished? Because I didn't have anything here yet. I don't have anything here. But I already know sort of how I'm going to come out of it because it's established. Okay, I'll show you what I mean. Something I do want to point out is a lot of times these baselines are going to tighten themselves up if you give it a session or two because something I noticed is it really wants to follow the vocal shot here. See how I have a hit there, but I don't have a hit here. And you can kind of feel that lack of space, but it's hard to get there when you're focusing on all this up here. But it's kind of obvious when I load up my next session, right? Like it it just wants to hit on the vocal shot. So that's something I'm going to add in later. And down here, you can see like I was just already doing it. [Music] So this is very stream of conscious and then it just kind of works itself out. So let's mute that for now and go into patterns. Okay. So remember these breaks mostly come from funk, right? Hip-hop a big evolution from funk among other genres, but funk is very important. Now, if this were a funk record and not a jungle track, it would be more like 90 BPM, right? So, while we have 64 bars of jungle, you know, we more so have 32 bars of like a groovy, easy to process tune, right? That's the important part. It the 32 bars is easier for the brain to process. Now, we're going to use that to kind of make it a little harder to process, which doesn't make a lot of sense, but we're just sending a lot of information out, right? But we're trying to find talking about it. Sounds really confusing and useless. So, let me just show you. So, we're going to go through our subdivisions just one at a time because it gets so confusing so fast when you just start throwing numbers around. Here's a bar. Not really complete, but two bars. That is complete. That's one bar in halftime. This is like one bar repeating itself for eight bars. Well, I have a fill here. So, seven bars repeated in a fill. It's not that I'm saying that while I write it, but when I zoom out, I can kind of I kind of look at that part too, right? It's not so much that there's a right or wrong here, but I I can like make sure that there's something to latch on to through that idea. Okay. So, let's start from here. [Music] See the it it really helps with the fills because you can get those two bar fills but you know it's one bar within an eight bar context which is 80 BPM. Now that allows us to use this very very popular D&B pattern. I got it from Stamina by the dream team. It's probably like my favorite track. I mean that it just goes up and up and up. Ah, so you can use this across D and B wherever two bars into one bar, right? Well, here let's just mute this. [Music] So, I'm just assuming you know the two-step jungle rhythm and D&B rhythm and stuff, right? You know, kit snare, kit snare, and then you got the 16th notes in there. Um, that's what's going on here across two bars in 80ish BPM. Here's my one, two, three, four, and then the back beat are my eighth notes, right? Isn't that cool? So, if you look, I have the two-step across this pattern. So, ignore Bitwig's native uh time markers. And look at mine where the plus signs are eighth notes. There's a snare on my two, there's a snare on my four, and on the end of my three, there's a kick, right? But this is stretched out across two bars, which means snare on five, which is like jungle superpower if you ask me. Snare on five. Why is it snare on five and not snare on one again? We're going back to those 80 BPM rhythms. I'm just going to say 80. If we put snare on one, it doesn't really do what we want it to do. It It's just not what we typically do. Now, that being said, if you have like a lead line of snares, you can put that wherever. I don't want to say it doesn't matter, but if you dial in the brakes, right, you can just layer kicks and snares from different breaks. It's disorienting and you can't just open up with it, but it works. You can work your way into it and, you know, kind of make the listener go, "Whoa, what's happening?" And it's because you're layering your kits and snares. But um that's something else. So that's how I kind of open the pattern up between these two 16 bar uh sections, right? I'll unmute this. [Music] And then I have a response to that part as well. I don't want to just repeat those two bars over and over, right? [Music] So, I didn't just go, I'll write this bar. I'll write this bar. I listen to this. [Music] And then I responded with this. And then, of course, the bass is having fun down there. [Music] [Music] So, we're through 32 bars. That's 16 bars and 80 BPM. Okay, here's our other 32. I copy and pasted the first eight bars of this, but then I changed up the last eight bars. So, I made that kind of more interesting with these snare hits down here. You see? Yeah. But here, I returned to the theme. [Music] That's good. [Music] Something else that's important here that really helps keep the track moving. I copied the break, changed up the baseline. All right. So, [Music] like right here, you can see I use that original baseline again. Sometimes you can get a little bit fatigued while making this, right? You can get a little tired. That's okay. It can be de It can be demotivating when you're like, "Oh, I need another 64 bars to keep going." Like that. It can be like, "Do I want to do this now?" And it's not a matter of like not enjoying it. It's just like it it's an uphill climb. That's okay. It's a fun uphill climb, but you have to start. And sometimes you just don't want to start again. So what I did was I copy and pasted half of a break, half of a session, and then I made a response to the section. Very musical, probably smarter and more dable than not doing so, right? It's it's balanced. Now, here is where I would get crazy. It's where I plan to get crazy. I'll show you what I mean. Remember that the listener has already heard this entire part right here. It's that is the second part. I'm honestly probably going to start [Music] right there. At this point, you have 16 bars to build up and then let go, right? And you can let go with stretch snares and just like weird glitchy bits and stuff like [Music] [Music] [Music] Hey, [Music] hey, hey. [Music] Hey, [Music] hey, hey. [Music] I'm a [Music] I'm a I'm a I'm a [Music] I'm a [Music] I'm a I'm a [Music] [Music] [Music] [Music] [Music] That could use some work right here. baseline. It's probably going to need changed, but you can see how this whole thing is a transition out of this. [Music] That actually sounds really good. I like it. We're keep it. [Music] Real quick, I just want to talk about how to analyze these more complicated bits, or at least how I do it When I'm driving around listening to Jungle or something, I'm not paying attention to the whole break. I'm paying attention to the backbeat and I'm paying attention to the down beat. And I'm switching my attention back and forth while counting to eight or 16, not four. You want to be able to see call and response while you're counting this. And you can say, you know what's happening with the three? Is there a snare on the five? That's always an important question. Is there a snare coming in on the five? Is there a snare coming in on the end of the five? Are heavy kits coming in on 16th notes? So remember, halftime within jungle and D&B is something you can exploit and it's not so much a rule you have to follow, but you can maintain balance and just continuous groove throughout more complicated bits when you utilize like an 80 BPM groove in a double time fashion pretty much. If you found this helpful, don't forget to check out the Patreon. Thanks for watching. Don't forget to like, comment, and subscribe. I'll see you next time.

web.markdown:
![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/JcDnUter0lU/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [Dorian, Phrygian & Aeolian Minor Scale Modes -Expert Level!](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JcDnUter0lU)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Joe Hanson Guitar](http://www.youtube.com/@joehansonguitar)
**Uploaded at**: 2022-12-19T09:00:10-08:00
**Published at**: 2022-12-19T09:00:10-08:00
**Length**: 05:45
**Views**: 849
**Likes**: 16
**Category**: Music

## Description

```
Should you use Dorian, Phrygian or Aeolian mode over minor chords? In this video I explain the role of minor chord function and how to determine which modes of the major scale will work best as well as which other types of minor scales work such as melodic minor, harmonic minor, minor pentatonic, etc.

Knowing this information will help you to better informed of the various choices you have when soloing over chords changes or even just coming up with an original melody.
#electricguitar #rockguitar #bluesguitar
```

## Transcript

hey what do you do when you're faced with a minor chord and you're supposed to play some sort of minor scale over it but you're not exactly sure which minor scale goes with that given minor chord if you know your major scale Theory which I'm sure most of you do you know that in any major scale there are three different minor scales derived from that or three different modes you've got Dorian phrygian and aeolian now how do you know which one of those three modes goes with that given chord if you're trying to keep everything diatonic meaning that it's from the same key as the chord progression today we're going to dive into what I call minor chord function and we're going to break down the different ways that we can analyze and determine which mode we should be using now many of you guys are busy and don't have hours in a day to sit around studying music theory and memorizing a bunch of key signatures and scale construction and cord relationships and things like that so I've created this mode Matrix right here and if you notice on the mode Matrix I've got all 12 major Keys as well as the names of all of the chords that are diatonic meaning they come from that scale from that parent key and then the names of all the modes associated with each one of those different chords so if we had a chord progression that when D Minor to f to a minor if I look at the top row I can see that in the key of C we've got a D Minor we've got an F and an A Minor and that set the second the fourth and the sixth modes right there or six second fourth and six degrees of the scale here's where it can get a little problematic for a lot of people is if we look down to the key of f we'll notice that at the sixth mode a only and we've got a D minor chord there we've also got an F as the one chord under ionian and then the third mode phrygian has got an A Minor so how do you know which scale should you use should you use Dorian minor over that chord progression or should you use aeolian mode over that chord progression generally it's a good idea to have a little more information meaning that if you see other chords in the progression such as what if we had a G minor chord and later on in that song chances are you're probably going to be playing D natural minor or the a only in mode from the key of f if we happen to see a G major chord or a G7 chord then pretty good indication that we're working with the key of C so you would use the Dorian minor scale over that one what if you're dealing with a chord progression that doesn't stay diatonic meaning it doesn't stay within one key I.E jazz or r b type songs where it's changing keys or we have some modulation and things like that or the overall chord progression just does not fit into one scale so you kind of have to use maybe a case-by-case basis as to which scale would you need to use over a given minor chord and sometimes it's up to your ears what sounds best to you over that so it's a good idea to experiment in the chord progression maybe Loop the chord progression or bring up a backing track on YouTube and then just practice soloing over it with the different minor scales and see which one fits the overall theme of the chord progression now would be a good time to hit that subscribe button and turn on those Bell notifications so you see when a new video is released there's also sometimes when the corporation and really doesn't have a strong harmonic movement to it meaning that it it doesn't really imply that it's from any one given key so once again at that point it's really up to your ears and your personal taste to determine which one of those scales would sound best over it now if you're faced with something like that you might find that the three different modes that are minor type scales from the major scale might not be the flavor you're looking for don't forget you've got melodic minor you've got harmonic minor there's a whole variety of other scales out there pentatonic minor you can do all kinds of different things with it but the purpose of this video was just to demonstrate minor chord function from major scale modes a lot of times when I see other guitar players or some of my students and they're improvising over chord progression that requires a minor scale 9 out of 10 times their go-to scale is the minor pentatonic which nothing wrong with minor pentatonic fantastic scale I use it all the time however there's some times when I want to be a little more sophisticated and add some extra color notes to my guitar solo so that's where knowing what that minor chord is functioning as in a given key will help me determine how I can apply a different mode to that minor chord to get some added melodic content out of that scale let me know what you think leave me a question or comment down below and don't forget to subscribe to Joseph hands and guitar studio turn on those Bell notifications so you see when the latest video comes out and check this video out right here until next time I'll see you guys later keep rocking on [Applause] [Music]

# Jungle/old school hardcore in the key of ?

- Thread starter [Cornish1999](https://www.dogsonacid.com/members/cornish1999.134506/)
- Start date [The Grid](https://www.dogsonacid.com/forums/the-grid/) \| [Dec 23, 2018](https://www.dogsonacid.com/threads/jungle-old-school-hardcore-in-the-key-of.797671/)

#### [Cornish1999](https://www.dogsonacid.com/members/cornish1999.134506/)

[![Cornish1999](https://production.dogsonacid.com/data/avatars/m/134/134506.jpg?1684279550)](https://www.dogsonacid.com/members/cornish1999.134506/)

3,163 Posts

As I get a bit more sophisticated in my productions I’m interested what Keys Jungle and old school is mainly in?

Thinking it through I can just wang some tracks through Traktor and see what the key is can’t I?

Interested in other Dogs thoughts on music theory and keys for Jungle

Cheers!

##### 1/2 Rogue Tone

[![thermal](https://production.dogsonacid.com/data/avatars/m/40/40351.jpg?1768758730)](https://www.dogsonacid.com/members/thermal.40351/)

18,490 Posts

Since Jan 27, 2005

I wouldn't worry too much about that although if you want a happy sound use major chords and otherwise try minor and stick to either type for all chords, e.g only major or only minor chords (sample a chord and play with it for proper oldskool sound).

That and be aware bass notes lower than D are difficult for sound systems to reproduce with punch. I think E is 41 Hz and try not to go lower than that.

- ![Like](https://storage.dogsonacid.com/smilies/like.png)

Reactions: [Rollin, Gdprbshsns, Cornish1999 and 1 other person](https://www.dogsonacid.com/posts/11992336/reactions)

[Dec 23, 2018](https://www.dogsonacid.com/threads/jungle-old-school-hardcore-in-the-key-of.797671/post-11992336)

#### [MVibe](https://www.dogsonacid.com/members/mvibe.133970/)

[![MVibe](https://production.dogsonacid.com/data/avatars/m/133/133970.jpg?1644107281)](https://www.dogsonacid.com/members/mvibe.133970/)

6,629 Posts

Since Jan 2, 2017

Just work in whatever key you're comfortable in, what tracks were you listening to? I can't say I've been aware of any specific key for anything but there was a thread about DnB in the key of 'F' but that was in reference to bass notes and it doesn't really make a great deal of sense because lots of notes are common to a lot of scales and it's kinda like putting the blinkers on. I think it's best not to even think about it. A lot of times, you don't even know what key you're in early on until an idea develops and even then you can be drawing from other keys for specific reasons.

I think for anyone to answer the question though, they'd have to first know a lot of tracks, then they'd have to know the key of the tracks and then they'd have to be able to tell which key pops up most frequently ![:D](https://storage.dogsonacid.com/smilies/biggrin.gif) By the time you did all that, you'd have prob made lots of tunes ![:slay2:](https://storage.dogsonacid.com/smilies/slay2.gif)

- ![Like](https://storage.dogsonacid.com/smilies/like.png)

Reactions: [Rollin, Cornish1999 and thermal](https://www.dogsonacid.com/posts/11992337/reactions)

[Dec 23, 2018](https://www.dogsonacid.com/threads/jungle-old-school-hardcore-in-the-key-of.797671/post-11992337)

#### [MVibe](https://www.dogsonacid.com/members/mvibe.133970/)

[![MVibe](https://production.dogsonacid.com/data/avatars/m/133/133970.jpg?1644107281)](https://www.dogsonacid.com/members/mvibe.133970/)

6,629 Posts

Since Jan 2, 2017

Yeah to echo what Thermal said, from my listening, most of that stuff isn't harmonically complex, not a load of Jazzy extentions or stuff like that, so pretty clunky major/minor chords are heard a lot. Triads, 7ths, possibly even 9ths but I think 7ths def crop up a lot. Don't be afraid of using out of scale chords either, esp with that 1 finger / sampled chord sound, that kind of wonkiness is pretty effective if that's what you are going for.

Name some tracks you like though, might be something were not thinking of

- ![Like](https://storage.dogsonacid.com/smilies/like.png)

Reactions: [Rollin and Cornish1999](https://www.dogsonacid.com/posts/11992339/reactions)

#### [Rollin](https://www.dogsonacid.com/members/rollin.131044/)

[![Rollin](https://production.dogsonacid.com/data/avatars/m/131/131044.jpg?1644258607)](https://www.dogsonacid.com/members/rollin.131044/)

4,962 Posts

Since Mar 6, 2016

A lot of the oldskool stuff isn't really made from a perspective of being in a particular key. More often it's based on samples transposed around in any way that sounds good.

From that perspective it doesn't matter what key stuff is in.

Having said that...

Although it takes a little practice to tell what key a song is in, the techniques to do so are really easy, so I think it is worth checking out a few youtube tutorials and exploring it a bit (30min of tutorials and a couple of hours practice should be plenty to get a good grasp of it).

This gives you the knowledge to be able to know which notes sound good when jamming over the top of existing songs.

Which I find fun and can give ideas and new ways of writing.

E.g. you find a sample you like. Before actually sampling it, jam over the top of the whole song (I.e. not just the sample in isolation). Discard the track, keep the jam, use bits that you like knowing they'll work with the sample (I'm oversimplifying a little but hopefully you get the gist).

I guess that getting traktor to do it should work, but I think you'll miss out some enjoyment that way.

- ![Like](https://storage.dogsonacid.com/smilies/like.png)

#### [Angela Merkya](https://www.dogsonacid.com/members/angela-merkya.118090/)

##### 非主流

[![Angela Merkya](https://production.dogsonacid.com/data/avatars/m/118/118090.jpg?1700975668)](https://www.dogsonacid.com/members/angela-merkya.118090/)

4,397 Posts

Since Oct 21, 2012

Seriously, get rid of the idea that there are regular keys for styles of music (like that "DnB in F thread")... it's a complete fabrication and it will just send you crazy rather than actually guide you towards writing a cogent piece of music.

Even more so for hardcore/early jungle: it used lots of sampled stabs/pads/chords, which creates parallel voicings (or whatever it's called). Therefore the idea that there is a 'key' in the traditional sense is irrelevant. The closest you can get is to pick some kind of common root note to anchor your bassline and synth riffs. It doesn't matter what that note is, as long as it makes you want to crack out your whistles, flares and get an undercut basin bouffant.

- ![Like](https://storage.dogsonacid.com/smilies/like.png)

Since Jan 2, 2017

There's still a lot of 'normal' scales / chord progressions but those quirky parallel ones are something I like too. Used to love that upfliting main riff in SL2 - DJ's Take Control ( sampled off The Nightwriters - Let The Music Use You ). That's a track that jumps out right away of mixing the one finger chord thing with a more normal chord sequence. That Landlord stab doing it's rounds in bits of the track. Where did the original 'Landlord' stab come from? M1 or something?

I think what's cool about some of these tracks and early Jungle and DnB is that they actually can be using different scales in different parts of the track, something you don't hear a lot today. Usually on more modern stuff, there's a theme and that theme is stuck to throughout the track. Wouldn't mind trying that kind of thing. My ears have a hard time detaching themselves from the previous part if I do stuff on a linear timeline because I'm always listening to it GOING INTO the next section so everything has to have continuity. In Ableton, could be useful to just make different scenes where you can switch off mentally from the previous scene and just switch up with something a bit more adventurous.

- ![Like](https://storage.dogsonacid.com/smilies/like.png)

The post you replied to was probably a joke, but in reality there is a such thing, but not in 12 notes per octave. There are few compositions from Renaissance Europe when split key keyboards were used that use additional notes that don't exist on later pianos/organs/harpischords. Arabs also use various note systems, some are close to 1/4 tones, some are way different.

It's interesting that modern tuning is way closer to Medieval system that was used from like 800 to 1400 than to the more refined system that was used up until early Romanticism (think 1800).

Many of the great classical works that we know sounded different, because of the better intonation (and different base pitch). Of course, you can't use unusual chord progressions in such system and not every tonality is avaliable (unless you have a keyboard with more than 12 pitches).

![Thumbnail (1920x1080)](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/GeDXUBsWPlw/maxresdefault.jpg)
# [A Beginner's Guide to Jungle Bass](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GeDXUBsWPlw)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Tim Cant](http://www.youtube.com/@TimCant)
**Uploaded at**: 2022-08-25T06:38:14-07:00
**Published at**: 2022-08-25T06:38:14-07:00
**Length**: 21:09
**Views**: 74497
**Likes**: 4560
**Category**: Howto & Style

## Description

```
A beginner’s guide to jungle bass. I kick off with a little context, then cover how to make some foundational jungle bass sounds with Serum.

I offer online tuition and music production assistance, book your session here: 
https://booktimcant.carrd.co/

Check out my DnB music on Spotify!
https://open.spotify.com/playlist/6P9o7d0n9U6hUuCWrpSEI6?si=bffb98f75e294953&nd=1

Check out my latest happy hardcore EPs:
https://endorrecordings.bandcamp.com/album/raveheart-ep-ndr019
https://endorrecordings.bandcamp.com/album/euphoric-rush-ep-ndr018

Chapter markers:

00:00 Intro

00:28 Chapter 1: The roots of jungle bass

02:14 Chapter 2: Basic waveforms

07:29 Chapter 3: 808 bass drums

11:27 Chapter 4: Reese bass

15:10 Chapter 5: Dred bass

18:01 Chapter 6: Wobble bass

Serum Patches:
https://drive.google.com/file/d/1Zoke3h0F0hIqxONm7flZ1h5iRsivwW3J/view?usp=sharing


Tunes mentioned:

Stranjah - Recreating Locust Reese By Ed Rush & Optical For 2020
https://youtu.be/GJagjDTfjU4

https://youtu.be/ayI4ghaG8Ls

https://youtu.be/Jh5Ls94nfF8


Jungle photo by Karl Anderson on Unsplash:
https://unsplash.com/@karlkiwi90
```

## Transcript

hey what's up tim kant here and in this video we're going to be looking and making some jungle bass sounds we're going to look at a bit of context and history but mainly this time we're going to be looking at some synthesis using serum but it's pretty simple synthesis so you should be able to recreate it in whatever synth you like links to the tracks mentioned and a whole load of other goodies are in the description so let's get rolling jungle breaks and jungle base both have a common ancestor in the american r b music of the mid 20th century jungle breaks came largely from funk which is a direct descendant of r b but jungle bass took a more circuitous route by jamaica r b got big in jamaica in the post war period and in the 50s the island evolved its own take on the sound scar in fact the famous 60s scar hit my boy lollipop by millie small is the cover of the less well-known 50s r b version by barbie gay in the late 60s an evolution of scar called reggae appeared reggae revolved around laid-back beats and big smoothed out electric bass jamaican sound systems had the power to blast out these big bass lines and this is where bass music as we know it today is born if you're a seasoned jungle enjoyer you'll likely recognize these reggae bass lines that have been sampled in one or more jungle tunes links to the tracks in the video description in the us in the late 80s another descendant of r b hip-hop spawned a sub-genre called miami base this is where us-based fetishization reached its zenith and you can hear pre-jungle pitched 808s in tracks like dynamics 2's just give the dj a break meanwhile in the uk acid house music had its own bass centric evolution in the form of bleep this took techno beats and added weighty dub reggae inspired bass lines that can be heard in tracks such as ability 2's pressure dub lfos lfo and nightmare on wax aftermath bleep was a direct precursor to hardcore and in hardcore bass lines you can hear influences from reggae miami bass and bleep many early jungle producers had a limited amount of kit that was sample based for example early akka hardware samplers or amigas running tracker software enterprising producers used akai signed test tones to create bass lines these were ideal for making heavy a hardcore bass because they had a high sustain level and being a sine wave without any harmonics above the fundamental frequency they left a lot of room in the mix for beefy breaks heavy kicks and full frequency stab sounds you can hear sign subs in hardcore jungle tunes such as nikki's give a little love and origin unknowns value of the shadows that said the akai testing does have a subtle touch of harmonics at the very start which we can hear and see with the help of voxengospan when i playback the sample of a sine test tone from an akai 3000 xl if you're looking for the utmost in authenticity a sample of an akai test tone is the way to go there's a link to an s950 sample in the description but we can get something pretty similar with serum i start with the default patch and set the first oscillator to the basic shapes wave table this gives us a sign by default and if we look at serums table editor we can see that this features just the fundamental harmonic very simple unlike a sampler the phase of the waveform is randomized which means that it starts from a different point each time by turning the random phase knob down to zero the oscillator will start from the same point in the waveform each time we can now control the part of the waveform the patch starts from with the phase knob at the origin we get a smooth sound but if we move the start point to near the peak or the trough of the sine cycle we get a little touch of attack a bit like the akitestone [Music] you can get the perfect balance of attack by selecting a specific point between the origin and the peak also you'll want to activate mono voicing so that you can't play more than one note at once a side sub occupies just the lowest part of the mix we can get a bigger field by adding some harmonics one way to do this is to activate sims filter and turn up the drive i feel like this is a little too much with the phase attack so i return the oscillator start point to the origin [Music] another way to introduce more harmonics is to use a waveform that already has them let's make a patch that uses a square wave oscillator we can see from serums wavetable editor that this sound has a lot more harmonics all the odd harmonics in fact and they roll off gradually [Music] this gives the square wave a very full sound a bit too full for a bass sound i'd argue so let's activate the filter with a low cutoff value we get something similar to a sine sub but with a bit more weight in the harmonics just above the fundamental [Music] if we click the keyboard icon the filter cutoff frequency will follow the note plate giving us some more consistent sound with a higher cut-off we get something closer to a reggae synth bass and this is something approaching the sort of bass lines you adhere in tracks like lenny the i swear ie and sl2 away in my brain [Music] [Music] make it open further and add a flanger you have something approaching awesome three headstrong [Music] here's a sub-base patch that uses a triangle which as we can see from serum's wave table editor again has odd harmonics but these roll off more quickly than the square waveform so this is a quick way to add just a touch more energy above the fundamental without using a filter or drive often hardcore producers would use pitch bends to create swooping bass lines and by setting serum's pitch bend range to plus minus 12 semitones we can get this very [Music] effect give this a touch of a mega star lo-fi you can apply some bit depth and sample rate reduction here i've used ableton live's redux effect with a bit depth of 8 and a sample rate of 6 kilohertz to add some noise and aliasing in the highs [Music] this is reminiscent of tracks such as urban shakedown some justice and foul play finest illusion 1993 as the nascent drama based style began to form the use of sampled roland's tr808 base jobs became increasingly popular 808 bass drums have a heavy sub component with a distinct pitch that lends them to being played chromatically and you can hear these kind of bass sounds in tracks like ltj becomes seminal music the invisible man the beginning and dj mayhem in s reiki 3 mix making an 808 base side is as simple as finding a suitable 808 bass drum sample loading into your sampler and tuning it [Music] i generally use synthesized 808s exclusively these days but back when i used to use 808 samples i found gold babies tape 808 to be an excellent source of 808 bass tones samples from mars also have a great collection of 808 sounds and you can find links to both of these in the video description tuning 808 can be tricky as they drop in pitch and this is what gives them their punchy percussive feel personally i like to unite weights by ear and to do this i pitched them up a few octaves and shooting them with a reference instrument [Music] [Music] if you take a listen to inessa reiki 3 mix you'll notice that the 808 bass sounds a touch distorted likely as a result of being run a little hot through a desk this adds harmonics and brings the sound into the mid-range this is something that producers would lean into as the jump up style of d b emerged and i believe you can hear this effect in tracks such as shyfx's this style and ma3 those djs one can achieve an approximation of analog distortion using a plugin such as soundtoys decapitator and is possible to create a wide variety of sounds using a single 808 sample and experimenting with the drive knob style mode and punish total [Music] for a more new skull sound you can try mixing some noise in with the 808 which will give you a crustier dirtier result [Music] as i mentioned earlier i tend to use synthesized weights for my own productions this is because i want a long loud sustain rather than the punchy relatively staccato sound of a real 808. to achieve this in serum i set up a sine oscillator turn the random phase down then assign lfo1 to the coarse pitch of oscillator a i change this modulation to unipolar in the modulation matrix so the patch doesn't go below the original frequency of the note i set lfo's one mod to envelope then create a fast drop down i turn off lfo 1's bpm sync so i can smoothly control the speed of the pitch job and i can control the amount of pitch modulation using the amount fader a real 808 would have the amplitude sustain level drop to zero giving it a really percussive feel but i keep the sustain level high to make the bass louder for longer in 1994 renegade aka ray keith's terrorist was released on moving shadow and it had a huge impact on the jungle scene with its reese baseline so called because it was sampled from reese aka techno pioneer kevin saundersons just won another chance they just want another chance bass is a long legato bass much like a sign sub but it also had a menacing pulsating yet smooth energy in the low mids in an interview i conducted for music radar kevin says he created the original reese using a casio cz series phase distortion synth but it's a relatively simple sound that we can certainly recreate in serum in fact i already did in a previous youtube video i'm a busy guy so let's just watch that video again yeah let's make a jungle reese with serum rc20 and pro-q we want two sawtooth oscillators detuned up and down 28 cents we run them through an 18db low-pass filter at 637 hertz with a resonance of 18 key tracking turned on and a drive level of 14 next we add rc20 with the distortion mode set to zap and a level of 64 percent with no flux plus we had some vinyl noise i suggest a level of 24 percent pro-q cuts at 350 hertz boosts at 150 hertz and there's a bit of high shelf to bring out the vinyl noise here's how it sounds compared to renegade terrorists [Music] i'm tim can please like and subscribe for more quick production tips all right a couple of areas i could have improved it one turning off the random face to give it a more consistent sound and two the authenticity of the result would have been improved by resampling a note and playing it back as a sample which would have made the rhythm of the swelling a touch more accurate here's what it sounds like if i make those changes [Music] the terrorist reese was sampled many times on tracks like djss's black remark rip dj high premix and amtrak by tracy nico by the tech step era producers began distorting the respace more and more and in ed rush optical and fierce's cut slow this arguably reached its apotheosis with the sound being distorted into a full frequency range filling monster by this point i think it's fair to say we've entered full-on drum and bass territory and here's where rhys becomes a byword for a huge detuned legato synth sound that's not just confined to the bass frequencies this also marks the start of where producers start splitting sounds into sub and top players which is a useful technique for not just sound design but also for making easier to get everything balanced in the mix i feel this is beyond the remit of the topic of this video so i've included links in the description for strangers recreating locust reese by ed russian optical for 2020 and fanu's create a healthy sub bass with serum fix the respace slash to oscillator sub bass drift problem which are great videos big up to those guys as we've examined the harmonic properties of sine square and triangle waveforms already let's see what sawtooth waveforms look like they have both odd and even harmonics which gives them a very full rich sound and this is why they're used for full frequency range sounds like reese's and supersouls later in 1994 another moving shadow release created another legendary bass sound this time it was the turn of dead dread with their anthem dread bass hardcore tracks often had low-pass filters closing over square waveforms to create a bass sound like this jedbase featured a low-pass filter opening over a square waveform giving it a reversed quality there had been a couple of tracks that predate dead dread that have had reversed bass lines specifically hyper experience lords of the null lines foul play featuring randall remix which has a delayed reverse base on the breakdown that's more of an effect rather than actual bass line and baby kane's jungle warriors which uses a reversed reggae bass line sample however dread bass was the first tune to really be focused around this type of sound its lazy sinister swells are bass contrasted with the high speed layered breaks and the dread bass sound became a staple of jungle and later speed garage most notably in double 99's rip groove making this sound is pretty straightforward we set oscillator 1 to a square waveform turn down the random phase then activate the filter and set its mode to mg low 24. this 24 db filter has a steeper roll-off than the default 12 db filter and this is a big part of what gives the patches sound activate filter pitch tracking and set the cutoff to 309 hertz [Music] at this point we're just hearing the sub frequencies because the filter cutoff is so low we need something to open it so i set lfo1 to modulate the filter cutoff frequency with a modulation amount of 46 i set the lfo motor envelope and turn off its bpm sync i set the rate to 2.3 hertz and now it's a match of setting up the envelope to get the right feel this is close but there's something missing what gives the dread bass sound its edge is resonance on the filter i set the resonance to 45 finally to beef things up i set the drive to 19 here's how it sounds compared to the original in 1996 wobble bass emerged as the hot new sound in tracks such as busy bee and marvelous cane featuring tempo o'neil every day and shim on an andy sea quest however this kind of bae sound can be heard in tracks as early as 1993's the trooper by dj hype which i feel was really ahead of its time in a few ways and there's a bit of an undirected gem busy b has a video demonstrating his take on the sound i'll link that in the description respect to busy bee here's my version in serum which uses volume modulation rather than detuning i start by setting oscillator onto a sine wave and turning down the random phase i then turn down the level of the oscillator to 23 this makes it very quiet but we're going to use an lfo to make it louder again i set lf01 to modulate the level knob with an amount of 69 i set the lfo to trigger with note start and turn off the bpm sync [Music] i want to saturate this sound a bit so i activate the filter turn the cut off all the way up and set the drive to 78 i also activate mono voicing so that i can only play one note at once so now we have a functional wobble base but the wobble is at the same tempo no matter what note we play i prefer it if the wobble changes tempo depending on the note plate as that gives us some rhythmic variety i set the lfo1 rate to 1 hertz in the modulation matrix i assign the note number to lfo1 rate with a modulation amount of 88 now higher notes will have a faster wobble and you can tweak things to your liking by adjusting the lfo1 rate and the note number to lfo1 rate modulation amount if you're looking to get the falling wobble style used in the 90s then it's a case of tweaking your pitch bend and lfo rate to get the perfect feel well that is more than enough jungle baseline farm for one video if you're feeling lazy or forgotten everything you've just seen there are some serum patches to download in the description other than that please like and subscribe and i'll catch you later
